response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been an electrician for over thirty years, mostly fixing wiring in homes across Philadelphia. It's a hands-on job that requires focus and attention to detail, which reminds me of my approach to handling things online. Dealing with fake content is like finding bad connections in electrical systems—it can be frustrating and cause problems if not addressed properly. I spend only about an hour each day on social media because it makes me anxious; seeing all the misinformation feels overwhelming at times. But as a married Christian man who's seen life through steady work rather than digital noise, I believe there are practical ways to manage this without getting too caught up.\n\nFirst thing is verification: just like when you're reading meter readings or blueprints, double-check everything with reliable sources before accepting it. If something looks suspiciously off—does it sound right? Has anyone else noticed inconsistencies? That's my gut telling me it might not be genuine. I've learned patience from building circuits; rushing leads to mistakes.\n\nAnother thing is limiting exposure but staying aware. My part-time job keeps things balanced, so spending just one hour online shouldn't derail anything important like family or church commitments. We attend services weekly in our Philadelphia neighborhood—real connection matters more than fake feeds.\n\nFinally, report what you find when it's clearly wrong to keep the platforms clean.",

Then I focused on practical steps: verification similar to checking electrical connections, using critical thinking skills from my experience, limiting time spent (since I'm anxious), and balancing digital life with real-world responsibilities given my part-time employment status.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been an electrician for over thirty years, mostly fixing wiring in homes across Philadelphia. It's a hands-on job that requires focus and attention to detail, which reminds me of my approach to handling things online. Dealing with fake content is like finding bad connections in electrical systems—it can be frustrating and cause problems if not addressed properly. I spend only about an hour each day on social media because it makes me anxious; seeing all the misinformation feels overwhelming at times. But as a married Christian man who's seen life through steady work rather than digital noise, I believe there are practical ways to manage this without getting too caught up.

First thing is verification: just like when you're reading meter readings or blueprints, double-check everything with reliable sources before accepting it. If something looks suspiciously off—does it sound right? Has anyone else noticed inconsistencies? That's my gut telling me it might not be genuine. I've learned patience from building circuits; rushing leads to mistakes.

Another thing is limiting exposure but staying aware. My part-time job keeps things balanced, so spending just one hour online shouldn't derail anything important like family or church commitments. We attend services weekly in our Philadelphia neighborhood—real connection matters more than fake feeds.

Finally, report what you find when it's clearly wrong to keep the platforms clean.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I started by relating my background as an electrician who deals with precise work and problems like handling fake content. I connected that analogy throughout my response. Then I focused on practical steps: verification similar to checking electrical connections, using critical thinking skills from my experience, limiting time spent (since I'm anxious), and balancing digital life with real-world responsibilities given my part-time employment status.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, grounded, cautious due to moderate anxiety levels
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as response focuses on personal strategies without citing or referencing any specific controversial sources.
